item.title: Leo’s Experimental Micrograntting Program 2026 | $10,000 Grants for Bold Ideas to Help Humanity Navigate AGI
item.dc:creator: Evelyn Essuman
item.description:
Have an ambitious idea that could genuinely help humanity navigate the future of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)? Leo’s Experimental Microgranting Program offers a refreshing alternative to traditional funding by providing $10,000 microgrants with minimal bureaucracy and maximum flexibility. Unlike conventional grants that require lengthy proposals and institutional affiliations, this initiative encourages applicants to spend about […]

The post Leo’s Experimental Micrograntting Program 2026 | $10,000 Grants for Bold Ideas to Help Humanity Navigate AGI appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Have an ambitious idea that could genuinely help humanity navigate the future of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)? Leo’s Experimental Microgranting Program offers a refreshing alternative to traditional funding by providing $10,000 microgrants with minimal bureaucracy and maximum flexibility.

Unlike conventional grants that require lengthy proposals and institutional affiliations, this initiative encourages applicants to spend about an hour explaining a thoughtful idea. The emphasis is on originality, genuine reasoning, and ambitious thinking rather than polished grant-writing or impressive credentials.

Leo’s program is an experimental grantmaking initiative designed to discover unconventional projects that could contribute to ensuring AGI benefits humanity.

About the Program

The Experimental Microgranting Program is built around one simple principle:

Actually try.

Applicants are encouraged to submit thoughtful, ambitious proposals instead of incremental academic work or applications filled with buzzwords.

Leo believes promising ideas can come from anyone and not only professors or established researchers – wants to support projects that traditional grantmakers may overlook.

The program is intentionally lightweight:

  • Approximately one-hour application
  • Maximum 1,000 words
  • Minimal bureaucracy
  • Flexible funding
  • Fast, independent decision-making

Grant Value

Successful applicants receive:

  • $10,000 USD
  • Flexible funding
  • Support for individuals, nonprofits, charities, or other entities
  • Opportunity to reapply after project completion

What Projects Are Being Funded?

The program seeks projects that could positively influence humanity’s future with AGI.

Examples include:

  • AI alignment research
  • AI governance ideas
  • Technical safety tools
  • Educational initiatives
  • Public awareness projects
  • Novel research
  • Policy work
  • Community initiatives
  • Experimental approaches with high upside

Applicants are encouraged to justify how their proposal contributes to helping humanity navigate AGI.

What Makes This Grant Different?

Unlike many traditional grants, this program offers:

  • Anonymous funder
  • Flexible use of funding
  • Learning is considered a successful outcome
  • Supports unconventional ideas
  • Values original thinking over credentials
  • Minimal administrative burden

The initiative itself is also an experiment and may evolve over time.

Application Guidelines

Applicants should carefully read the complete guidance before applying.

Important expectations include:

  • Spend around one hour preparing the application.
  • Write clearly.
  • Explain why the project matters.
  • Avoid exaggerated claims.
  • Avoid unnecessary buzzwords.
  • Submit genuine work.
  • Applications written or edited by AI will be rejected.
  • Multiple ideas may be submitted separately.

Leo specifically emphasizes that applications should reflect the applicant’s own reasoning and writing.

Eligibility

The program welcomes applicants from diverse backgrounds.

Applicants do not need:

  • Previous AI alignment experience
  • Academic publications
  • Prestigious university affiliations
  • Extensive grant-writing experience

Strong ideas and thoughtful reasoning matter far more than credentials.

Funding Philosophy

Rather than expecting every project to succeed, the program views experimentation as valuable.

Even projects that produce negative results may still be considered successful if they generate meaningful learning.

Once grant funds have been used, recipients may apply again by providing a progress update.

Referral Bonus

Applicants who refer others can receive a $100 referral bounty if the referred applicant is awarded a microgrant.

Important Notes

Applicants should note:

  • Grants should not be used as credentials or endorsements.
  • Leo may seek external reviewers.
  • Grant decisions are highly selective.
  • Funding availability is expected to support all successful applications.
  • The program operates independently from Leo’s employer.

How to Apply

Applicants should first read the complete guidance document before completing the application form.

Application Form:
https://tally.so/r/D4WlbR

Questions:
alignmentmicrogrants@gmail.com

Conclusion

Leo’s Experimental Microgranting Program offers a refreshing alternative to traditional grant funding by placing trust in bold ideas, genuine reasoning, and intellectual curiosity rather than lengthy proposals or prestigious credentials. With a straightforward application process and flexible $10,000 funding, the program encourages individuals and organizations to pursue ambitious projects that could contribute to a safer and more beneficial future with Artificial General Intelligence (AGI).

Whether your proposal focuses on AI safety, governance, education, public engagement, technical research, or another innovative approach, this initiative welcomes unconventional thinking and recognizes that meaningful progress often begins with experimental ideas. Applicants are encouraged to write honestly, explain why their project matters, and demonstrate their own original thinking without relying on polished grant-writing or AI-generated content.

If you have a well-considered idea that could help humanity navigate the opportunities and challenges of AGI, this microgrant program provides an excellent opportunity to secure funding and turn that vision into action. Be sure to read the full application guidance carefully before applying, follow all instructions, and submit your proposal through the official application form. Even if your project does not achieve every intended outcome, the program values learning and meaningful insights, making it an ideal opportunity for innovators who are willing to experiment, take thoughtful risks, and contribute to shaping a positive future for humanity.

Discover more opportunities like these on the OFY webpage .

The post Leo’s Experimental Micrograntting Program 2026 | $10,000 Grants for Bold Ideas to Help Humanity Navigate AGI appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026: Apply to Shape Global Education Governance and Represent Crisis-Affected Youth
item.dc:creator: Fridah Kamuri
item.description:
Education Cannot Wait (ECW), the United Nations global fund dedicated to education in emergencies and protracted crises, has officially opened applications for the 2026 ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election Call. This unique global opportunity invites eligible youth-led and student-led organizations to become part of an influential international constituency that helps shape ECW’s […]

The post Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026: Apply to Shape Global Education Governance and Represent Crisis-Affected Youth appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Education Cannot Wait (ECW), the United Nations global fund dedicated to education in emergencies and protracted crises, has officially opened applications for the 2026 ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election Call. This unique global opportunity invites eligible youth-led and student-led organizations to become part of an influential international constituency that helps shape ECW’s governance, policies, and strategic priorities.

Far more than a standard membership opportunity, the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group provides youth organizations with a direct platform to influence global decision-making in education, humanitarian response, and crisis recovery. Selected organizations will have opportunities to contribute to policy consultations, engage with international education stakeholders, vote in governance elections, and even seek election to leadership positions within ECW’s governance structure.

For youth organizations committed to advancing education for children and young people affected by conflict, displacement, disasters, and other crises, this represents an exceptional opportunity to amplify their voices on the global stage while helping shape the future of education in emergencies.

What is the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group?

The ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group is a global constituency established to ensure that the voices, experiences, and priorities of young people and crisis-affected communities are reflected throughout Education Cannot Wait’s governance and decision-making processes.

Recognizing that young people are not merely beneficiaries of education programmes but active partners in designing solutions, ECW created this platform to strengthen youth participation in shaping international education policies and investments.

The Sub-group brings together youth-led and student-led organizations from around the world that are actively working in education, humanitarian response, and crisis contexts. Through this network, members contribute valuable perspectives that influence ECW’s policies, governance, and strategic direction.

Why This Opportunity Matters

Millions of children and young people worldwide face interrupted education because of armed conflict, climate change, forced displacement, political instability, and humanitarian emergencies.

Education Cannot Wait works to ensure that quality education remains accessible during these crises. However, sustainable solutions require meaningful participation from the young people and organizations directly affected by these challenges.

The Youth and Student-led Sub-group ensures that youth voices remain central to discussions about:

  • Education in emergencies.
  • Humanitarian response.
  • Recovery from crises.
  • Inclusive education policies.
  • Long-term resilience.
  • Youth participation in governance.
  • Global education financing.

By joining the Sub-group, organizations become active contributors to international conversations that influence education policy worldwide.

Objectives of the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group

The initiative aims to:

  • Strengthen youth representation within ECW governance.
  • Ensure crisis-affected communities influence policy decisions.
  • Promote meaningful youth participation in international education discussions.
  • Build stronger collaboration between youth organizations and global education partners.
  • Increase accountability through inclusive decision-making.
  • Foster partnerships among youth-led organizations working in emergency education.
  • Support leadership development within the global education sector.

Benefits of Joining the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group

Successful organizations receive access to numerous professional, networking, and governance opportunities.

These include:

1. Participate in Global Policy Consultations

Members contribute directly to consultations regarding ECW’s:

  • Strategic priorities.
  • Policies.
  • Programmes.
  • Governance decisions.
  • Future initiatives.

Their perspectives help ensure that youth experiences remain central to global education planning.

2. Influence International Decision-Making

One of the most valuable aspects of membership is the opportunity to influence international education governance.

Members provide recommendations and insights that shape ECW’s work supporting children and youth affected by crises.

3. Build International Networks

Organizations become part of a worldwide community of professionals and youth organizations working in:

  • Education.
  • Humanitarian assistance.
  • Emergency response.
  • Child protection.
  • Youth leadership.
  • Community development.

These connections encourage collaboration and knowledge sharing across regions.

4. Participate in Governance Elections

Members have voting rights in elections for representatives serving on ECW governance bodies.

This enables organizations to participate in selecting leaders who will represent youth interests within the institution.

5. Opportunity to Seek Leadership Positions

Eligible organizations may not only vote but also stand for election.

During the 2026 membership cycle, organizations may seek election to represent the constituency on:

  • High-Level Steering Group (HLSG).
  • Executive Committee (ExCom).

Successful candidates will serve a two-year mandate, representing youth voices within ECW’s highest governance structures.

Who Can Apply?

Applications are open to eligible organizations that meet ECW’s membership requirements.

Applicants must be:

  • Youth-led organizations.
  • Student-led organizations.
  • Not-for-profit organizations.
  • Organizations working in education.
  • Organizations supporting education in emergencies.
  • Organizations working within humanitarian or crisis settings.

Applicants should carefully review the official Membership & Election Guidance Note for complete eligibility criteria before submitting their application.

Leadership Opportunities Available

Membership also provides organizations with the opportunity to play a leadership role within ECW.

Representatives elected to the High-Level Steering Group or Executive Committee contribute to:

  • Strategic planning.
  • Governance oversight.
  • Representation of youth interests.
  • Policy discussions.
  • Organizational accountability.

These leadership roles help ensure that youth-led organizations have meaningful influence over ECW’s future direction.

Key Dates

Applicants should take note of the important deadlines:

  • Membership applications open: 6 July 2026
  • Deadline for membership applications and election nominations: 26 July 2026
  • Voting period: 3–5 August 2026
  • Announcement of elected representatives: 7 August 2026

How to Apply

Eligible organizations should complete the official application process by following these steps:

  1. Complete the Membership Application Form.
  2. Submit the Election Nomination Form if seeking election.
  3. Download and carefully review the Membership & Election Guidance Note.
  4. Ensure all eligibility requirements are met before submission.
  5. Submit the application before the deadline.

APPLY HERE 

Why Organizations Should Apply

Membership in the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group offers far more than recognition. It provides organizations with a genuine opportunity to shape international education governance while representing the interests of young people affected by crises around the world.

Participating organizations gain access to influential policy discussions, strengthen their international partnerships, contribute to global education solutions, and help ensure that youth perspectives remain central to humanitarian and development responses.

For organizations seeking to expand their impact beyond national borders, strengthen their leadership profile, and influence the future of education in emergencies, this opportunity offers an exceptional platform for meaningful engagement.

Visit HERE to learn more about the Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026

Application Deadline

26 July 2026

Applications and election nominations must be submitted by this deadline.

Conclusion

The Education Cannot Wait Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026 Membership and Election Call is a remarkable opportunity for eligible youth-led and student-led organizations committed to advancing education in emergencies and supporting crisis-affected communities. Through membership, organizations can actively participate in global policy consultations, contribute to ECW’s governance, build international partnerships, vote in leadership elections, and even stand for influential positions on the High-Level Steering Group and Executive Committee. If your organization is passionate about youth leadership, education, and humanitarian action, this is an opportunity to help shape the future of global education governance while ensuring that the voices of young people remain at the heart of decision-making.

Application Deadline: 26 July 2026.

For more global Fellowship opportunities, visit OFY HERE

The post Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026: Apply to Shape Global Education Governance and Represent Crisis-Affected Youth appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program 2026: Prestigious Fellowship for Journalists, Technologists, Policymakers, Scholars, and Media Professionals
item.dc:creator: Priyanka Kharbanda
item.description:
The Shorenstein Fellows Program at the Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy, housed within Harvard Kennedy School, is one of the world’s leading fellowship opportunities for professionals and scholars working at the intersection of media, technology, politics, and public policy. For nearly four decades, the program has served as a hub for influential […]

The post Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program 2026: Prestigious Fellowship for Journalists, Technologists, Policymakers, Scholars, and Media Professionals appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Shorenstein Fellows Program at the Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy, housed within Harvard Kennedy School, is one of the world’s leading fellowship opportunities for professionals and scholars working at the intersection of media, technology, politics, and public policy.

For nearly four decades, the program has served as a hub for influential thinkers, journalists, policymakers, technologists, filmmakers, creators, and researchers who are shaping how information is produced, distributed, and understood in democratic societies.

Founded in 1986, the fellowship has welcomed more than 300 distinguished fellows from around the world. These fellows have contributed groundbreaking research, influential publications, public discussions, documentaries, policy recommendations, and innovative projects that address some of the most pressing challenges affecting today’s global information ecosystem.

The Shorenstein Fellows Program offers selected participants the opportunity to pursue an independent project while becoming part of Harvard Kennedy School’s vibrant intellectual community. Fellows collaborate with leading scholars and practitioners while producing original work that informs public debate and strengthens democratic institutions.

Whether your expertise lies in journalism, artificial intelligence, technology policy, digital governance, media innovation, political communication, public leadership, or academic research, the Shorenstein Fellows Program provides a prestigious platform to expand your ideas and contribute to global conversations.

About the Shorenstein Center

The Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy is a research center at Harvard Kennedy School dedicated to studying how information shapes societies, governments, and democratic institutions.

Its work focuses on understanding:

  • Journalism and news media.
  • Digital technology.
  • Artificial intelligence.
  • Public policy.
  • Political communication.
  • Information integrity.
  • Democracy.
  • Civic engagement.
  • The evolving global information ecosystem.

Through research, fellowships, publications, events, and collaborations, the Center seeks to improve the creation, distribution, and consumption of trustworthy information worldwide.

About the Shorenstein Fellows Program

The Shorenstein Fellows Program is designed to bring together professionals and scholars from multiple disciplines who are interested in examining critical issues surrounding media, politics, technology, and governance.

The fellowship emphasizes independent scholarship while encouraging collaboration within Harvard’s academic environment.

Participants pursue self-directed projects that contribute meaningful insights into the changing landscape of information, democracy, and public communication.

Throughout the fellowship, fellows engage with faculty, researchers, students, and fellow practitioners while sharing expertise from their own professional backgrounds.

Purpose of the Fellowship

The fellowship aims to:

  • Advance public understanding of media and democracy.
  • Support innovative research on technology and public policy.
  • Encourage interdisciplinary collaboration.
  • Promote thoughtful analysis of today’s information ecosystem.
  • Foster dialogue between practitioners and academics.
  • Produce research that informs policymakers and the general public.
  • Strengthen democratic institutions through better understanding of information flows.

Areas of Focus

The fellowship welcomes projects exploring a broad range of topics, including:

  • Journalism
  • Digital media
  • Artificial intelligence
  • Social media
  • Information integrity
  • Public policy
  • Political communication
  • Democracy
  • Technology governance
  • Election integrity
  • Misinformation and disinformation
  • Civic engagement
  • Digital platforms
  • Media ethics
  • Public interest storytelling
  • Documentary filmmaking
  • Government innovation
  • Internet governance
  • Freedom of expression
  • Data and society

Interdisciplinary proposals that combine multiple fields are particularly encouraged.

What Fellows Do

During the fellowship, participants pursue an independent project while actively engaging with the Shorenstein Center community.

Projects may include:

  • Academic research
  • Policy papers
  • Opinion articles
  • Essays
  • Investigative journalism
  • Reports
  • Webinars
  • Documentary storytelling
  • Multimedia projects
  • Public presentations
  • Books or book chapters
  • Digital publications

The fellowship encourages work that translates complex ideas into accessible content for both policymakers and the general public.

Contribution to the Research Community

Beyond completing their own projects, fellows contribute to the broader intellectual life of the Center by:

  • Participating in discussions.
  • Sharing expertise with faculty and students.
  • Collaborating across disciplines.
  • Presenting research findings.
  • Engaging in seminars and public events.
  • Supporting dialogue around contemporary media and policy challenges.

This collaborative environment allows fellows to benefit from diverse perspectives while expanding the impact of their own work.

Distinguished Fellowship Community

Since its establishment, the Shorenstein Fellows Program has hosted an impressive community of professionals representing journalism, technology, government, academia, filmmaking, and policy.

The Center has welcomed over 300 fellows since 1986, reflecting its longstanding commitment to fostering thought leadership across multiple sectors.

Recent fellows have included:

  • Journalists
  • Documentary filmmakers
  • Artificial intelligence experts
  • Technology leaders
  • Public policy specialists
  • Government officials
  • Academic researchers
  • Digital innovation experts
  • Media executives

This diverse fellowship community creates valuable opportunities for networking, collaboration, and intellectual exchange.

Research and Publications

Fellows contribute to an extensive body of work addressing today’s most important challenges surrounding media and democracy.

Examples of publications include:

  • Research reports
  • Policy papers
  • Commentary articles
  • Explainers
  • Public webinars
  • Essays
  • Original journalism

Recent topics have explored:

  • Artificial intelligence in journalism
  • Media consumption
  • Government technology
  • Digital democracy
  • Information integrity
  • Emerging technologies
  • Public trust

These publications help inform policymakers, researchers, journalists, and the broader public.

Why the Fellowship Is Prestigious

The Shorenstein Fellows Program is internationally respected because it combines:

  • Harvard Kennedy School’s academic excellence.
  • Independent research opportunities.
  • Interdisciplinary collaboration.
  • Public policy engagement.
  • Global visibility.
  • Access to leading experts.
  • Opportunities to influence contemporary debates.

For professionals working in media, technology, journalism, or public policy, the fellowship represents an opportunity to significantly expand both research impact and professional networks.

Benefits of the Shorenstein Fellows Program

Although individual fellowship arrangements may vary, participants generally benefit from:

  • Affiliation with Harvard Kennedy School.
  • Membership in an internationally recognized research community.
  • Opportunities to pursue independent research.
  • Collaboration with distinguished scholars and practitioners.
  • Access to seminars, discussions, and academic events.
  • A platform to publish original work.
  • Increased visibility within media and policy circles.
  • Professional networking opportunities.
  • Intellectual exchange across multiple disciplines.
  • Long-term association with an influential fellowship network.

Who Should Consider Applying?

This fellowship is ideal for professionals who wish to explore important issues affecting today’s information ecosystem.

Strong candidates may include:

  • Journalists
  • Editors
  • Researchers
  • Professors
  • Public policy experts
  • Political advisors
  • Government officials
  • Technology professionals
  • AI researchers
  • Documentary filmmakers
  • Media entrepreneurs
  • Communication specialists
  • Civic technology leaders
  • Digital platform researchers

Apply here

Why This Fellowship Matters Today

The rapid evolution of technology, artificial intelligence, digital media, and political communication has transformed how societies access and evaluate information.

The Shorenstein Fellows Program supports research that addresses these changes while promoting evidence-based dialogue and innovative solutions.

By bringing together experts from diverse backgrounds, the fellowship encourages collaboration on issues that influence democratic governance, journalism, and public trust worldwide.

For more details please visit here 

Application Deadline

No application deadline is provided in the available information.

Prospective applicants should regularly monitor the Shorenstein Center’s official fellowship page for future application announcements, eligibility updates, and submission deadlines.

Conclusion

The Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program is one of the world’s premier fellowships for professionals and scholars dedicated to improving the understanding of media, technology, politics, and public policy. By providing an intellectually rich environment where independent research, interdisciplinary collaboration, and public engagement thrive, the fellowship empowers participants to produce meaningful work with lasting societal impact. With its distinguished global network, longstanding reputation, and commitment to advancing knowledge about today’s evolving information ecosystem, the program offers an exceptional opportunity for journalists, technologists, policymakers, scholars, and creators seeking to shape the future of media and democracy. Interested applicants should monitor the official fellowship announcements for future application deadlines and program updates.

For more fellowship opportunities please visit OFY here

The post Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program 2026: Prestigious Fellowship for Journalists, Technologists, Policymakers, Scholars, and Media Professionals appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Methodology PhD Studentships in Maternity Disparities 2027 in the UK: Fully Funded NIHR PhD Opportunities for Researchers Passionate About Maternal Health Equity
item.dc:creator: Richard Nyirenda
item.description:
Persistent inequalities in maternity care continue to affect the health and wellbeing of women and babies across the United Kingdom. Addressing these disparities requires innovative research, skilled researchers, and multidisciplinary collaboration. To help build the next generation of experts in this critical field, the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) Inequalities Challenge: Maternity […]

The post Methodology PhD Studentships in Maternity Disparities 2027 in the UK: Fully Funded NIHR PhD Opportunities for Researchers Passionate About Maternal Health Equity appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Persistent inequalities in maternity care continue to affect the health and wellbeing of women and babies across the United Kingdom. Addressing these disparities requires innovative research, skilled researchers, and multidisciplinary collaboration. To help build the next generation of experts in this critical field, the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) Inequalities Challenge: Maternity Disparities Consortium (MDC) has launched its Methodology PhD Studentships Competition for 2027.

The Consortium is offering up to 13 fully funded PhD studentships across multiple universities within its UK-wide network. These studentships are designed to develop future research leaders who can generate evidence-based solutions that reduce inequalities before, during, and after pregnancy.

This opportunity is particularly suitable for applicants with expertise in research methodologies such as statistics, health economics, implementation science, digital research, data science, trial design, co-production methods, and related disciplines who are passionate about improving maternal health outcomes.

Whether you are an aspiring academic, healthcare professional, statistician, economist, data scientist, or researcher from another methodological background, this programme provides an exceptional opportunity to contribute to meaningful research while earning a fully funded doctoral degree.

About the NIHR Inequalities Challenge: Maternity Disparities Consortium (MDC)

The Maternity Disparities Consortium (MDC) is a major UK-wide collaboration established under the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) to address longstanding inequalities in maternity care.

The Consortium brings together a diverse partnership of:

  • Universities
  • NHS Trusts
  • Local councils
  • Charities
  • Industry partners
  • Research organisations

By combining expertise from these sectors, the Consortium aims to develop practical, evidence-based solutions that improve maternity outcomes for women and babies throughout pregnancy and beyond.

The Consortium is committed to conducting inclusive research that reflects the experiences of diverse communities while ensuring that research findings lead to real improvements in healthcare services.

Why This PhD Programme Matters

Despite significant advances in healthcare, inequalities in maternity outcomes remain a major challenge in the UK. Certain communities continue to experience poorer maternal and neonatal outcomes due to social, economic, ethnic, and healthcare-related disparities.

The Methodology PhD Studentships aim to strengthen research capacity by training specialists who can develop innovative research methods capable of addressing these complex issues.

Successful students will become part of a national research community working towards improving maternal health through rigorous scientific investigation and interdisciplinary collaboration.

Capacity Development Programme

A central objective of the Maternity Disparities Consortium is to build sustainable research capacity across the United Kingdom.

The Consortium’s Capacity Development Programme seeks to:

  • Develop the next generation of maternity disparities researchers.
  • Build long-term expertise in methodological research.
  • Strengthen the UK’s maternity research workforce.
  • Support future research leaders.
  • Encourage collaboration across healthcare, academic, clinical, and community settings.
  • Increase research participation from underrepresented disciplines.
  • Promote inclusive research environments.
  • Deliver lasting improvements in maternal healthcare.

The programme also contributes to important UK government priorities, including:

  • Women’s Health Strategy for England.
  • 10-Year Health Plan for England.

Through this initiative, the Consortium hopes to create a stronger, more diverse workforce capable of delivering meaningful improvements in maternal healthcare for years to come.

PhD Studentship Opportunities

The Consortium will fund up to 13 PhD studentships, each based at one of the participating universities within the Consortium.

Projects will focus on reducing maternity inequalities using advanced research methodologies.

Additional research projects are expected to be announced throughout the recruitment period.

Applicants are encouraged to review individual project descriptions before applying, as each project may have its own specific requirements.

Research Areas Being Sought

The Consortium is particularly interested in applicants with methodological expertise in areas such as:

  • Health Economics
  • Statistics
  • Trial Design
  • Data Science
  • Digital Research
  • Implementation Science
  • Co-production Methods

Applicants from other methodological backgrounds are also encouraged to apply if they believe their skills can contribute to maternity inequalities research.

The Consortium values interdisciplinary approaches and welcomes innovative ideas that can improve maternal and neonatal outcomes.

Programme Duration

The PhD studentships will begin in:

January 2027

Students may choose one of the following study options where available:

  • Full-time study for up to 3 years.
  • Part-time study (0.8 Whole-Time Equivalent) for up to 4 years.

Some research projects may only be offered on a full-time basis. Applicants should carefully check the requirements for each individual project before submitting an application.

Funding Package

Successful applicants will receive a comprehensive funding package that includes:

Full Tuition Fees

The studentship covers:

  • Full UK home tuition fees at the 2026/27 rate.

Annual Tax-Free Stipend

Students will receive a tax-free maintenance stipend at UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) rates:

  • £21,805 per year for students based outside London.
  • £23,805 per year for students based in London.

These rates apply from 1 October 2026.

Research Support Funding

Each award includes:

  • £5,000 towards:
    • Research expenses
    • Training
    • Travel
    • Conference attendance
    • Consumables

This additional funding enables students to undertake high-quality research while developing their professional skills.

International Student Information

International applicants are welcome to apply.

However, applicants should note that:

  • The studentship only covers tuition fees at the UK Home rate.
  • International students must independently secure funding to cover the difference between Home and Overseas tuition fees.
  • Students must satisfy admission requirements set by their host university.

Applicants are advised to consult the postgraduate admissions requirements of their chosen university before applying.

Who Should Apply?

The Consortium welcomes applications from individuals who are passionate about improving maternal health equity through high-quality research.

Applicants should have methodological knowledge or experience relevant to one or more of the research areas being supported.

The Consortium is especially interested in individuals who can contribute fresh perspectives and innovative research approaches.

Applicants from diverse academic and professional backgrounds are encouraged to demonstrate how their previous experience will strengthen maternity inequalities research.

Where this is not immediately apparent, applicants should clearly explain in their cover letter how their disciplinary expertise is relevant to their proposed PhD project.

APPLY HERE

Commitment to Equality, Diversity and Inclusion

The Consortium is committed to creating an inclusive, supportive and caring research environment.

Applications are particularly encouraged from:

  • People from underrepresented ethnic backgrounds.
  • People living with disabilities.
  • Individuals with neurodivergent conditions.
  • People with long-term health requirements.
  • Applicants of all genders.
  • Applicants of all ages.
  • Applicants of all beliefs.

By promoting diversity within its research community, the Consortium aims to produce research that better reflects the populations it serves.

Why You Should Consider Applying

This studentship offers numerous benefits, including:

  • Fully funded doctoral study.
  • Tax-free annual living allowance.
  • Additional research funding.
  • Opportunities to collaborate with leading UK universities and NHS organisations.
  • Participation in nationally significant health research.
  • Access to multidisciplinary research expertise.
  • Professional development and advanced research training.
  • Opportunities to become a future leader in maternal health research.
  • Contribution to reducing inequalities affecting women and babies.

Important Information for Applicants

Before applying, applicants should remember to:

  • Review individual PhD project descriptions.
  • Confirm eligibility with the host university.
  • Check postgraduate admission requirements.
  • Prepare a strong cover letter explaining their methodological expertise.
  • Demonstrate how their experience aligns with maternity disparities research.
  • Verify funding arrangements if applying as an international student.

Deadline

No application deadline has been announced yet.

Applicants are encouraged to monitor the Consortium’s official announcements regularly, as additional PhD projects will continue to be added and application timelines may vary by host university.

Visit HERE to learn more about the Methodology PhD Studentships in Maternity Disparities 2027 in the UK

Conclusion

The NIHR Inequalities Challenge: Maternity Disparities Consortium Methodology PhD Studentships 2027 represent an outstanding opportunity for aspiring researchers who want to make a meaningful contribution to maternal health equity. With up to 13 fully funded doctoral positions, generous financial support, and access to a nationwide network of leading universities, NHS Trusts, councils, charities, and industry partners, this programme provides an exceptional platform for developing advanced research skills while addressing one of the UK’s most important public health challenges.

The Consortium is not only investing in doctoral education but also building a diverse and inclusive research community capable of delivering long-term improvements in maternity care. If you have expertise in research methodology and a passion for improving outcomes for women and babies, this programme offers an excellent opportunity to develop your academic career while creating lasting social impact. Prospective applicants should begin preparing their application materials early, review individual project requirements carefully, and monitor official updates for newly released projects and application deadlines.

For more international PhD opportunities, visit OFY HERE

The post Methodology PhD Studentships in Maternity Disparities 2027 in the UK: Fully Funded NIHR PhD Opportunities for Researchers Passionate About Maternal Health Equity appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions (MSCA) Postdoctoral Fellowships 2026: Fully Funded Research Fellowships in Europe for International PhD Researchers
item.dc:creator: Ling Peng
item.description:
The Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions (MSCA) Postdoctoral Fellowships 2026 are now open for applications, offering one of the world’s most prestigious and competitive funding opportunities for postdoctoral researchers. Funded under the European Union’s Horizon Europe Programme, these fellowships are designed to support outstanding researchers who wish to conduct high-quality research abroad, develop new skills, expand international […]

The post Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions (MSCA) Postdoctoral Fellowships 2026: Fully Funded Research Fellowships in Europe for International PhD Researchers appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions (MSCA) Postdoctoral Fellowships 2026 are now open for applications, offering one of the world’s most prestigious and competitive funding opportunities for postdoctoral researchers. Funded under the European Union’s Horizon Europe Programme, these fellowships are designed to support outstanding researchers who wish to conduct high-quality research abroad, develop new skills, expand international collaborations, and advance their academic or non-academic careers.

The programme encourages international mobility and interdisciplinary research while strengthening Europe’s research and innovation ecosystem. Whether you are looking to move within Europe, relocate to Europe from another region of the world, or gain international experience outside Europe before returning, the MSCA Postdoctoral Fellowships provide generous financial support and access to world-class research environments.

Application Deadline: 9 September 2026 (17:00 CEST)

What are the Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions Postdoctoral Fellowships?

The Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions (MSCA) are among the flagship funding programmes of the European Commission that support researcher mobility, training, and career development.

The Postdoctoral Fellowships (PFs) specifically target researchers who already hold a PhD and are looking to:

  • Develop independent research careers
  • Conduct research in another country
  • Learn new scientific techniques
  • Build interdisciplinary expertise
  • Strengthen international research collaborations
  • Gain exposure to both academic and non-academic sectors
  • Improve long-term career prospects

Rather than simply funding a research project, the programme focuses on developing the researcher through international experience, advanced training, networking opportunities, and career progression.

Postdoctoral Fellowships in research areas covered by the Euratom Research and Training Programme

Why Apply for the MSCA Postdoctoral Fellowships?

The fellowship provides far more than financial support. It enables researchers to become internationally competitive while working with leading universities, research institutes, companies, SMEs, and innovation organizations.

Successful fellows benefit from:

  • Fully funded research positions
  • International mobility opportunities
  • Career development support
  • Advanced research training
  • Cross-sector collaboration
  • Networking with leading scientists
  • Improved employability
  • International research experience
  • Opportunities to work in both academic and industry settings

Types of MSCA Postdoctoral Fellowships

The programme offers two distinct fellowship schemes, allowing researchers to choose the pathway that best fits their career goals.

1. European Postdoctoral Fellowships

European Postdoctoral Fellowships support researchers who wish to carry out research in:

  • European Union (EU) Member States, or
  • Horizon Europe Associated Countries.

Researchers may:

  • Move from one European country to another
  • Relocate from anywhere in the world to Europe

Duration

The fellowship lasts:

  • 1–2 years

Who can apply?

Researchers of any nationality are eligible to apply, provided they satisfy the eligibility requirements.

2. Global Postdoctoral Fellowships

Global Postdoctoral Fellowships support researchers wishing to gain experience outside Europe before returning.

The fellowship includes two phases:

Outgoing Phase

Researchers spend:

  • 1–2 years
  • At a host organisation located in a non-associated Third Country outside Europe.

Return Phase

Researchers then complete:

  • A mandatory 1-year return phase
  • At an organisation located in an EU Member State or Horizon Europe Associated Country.

Total Duration

  • 2–3 years

Who can apply?

Only researchers who are:

  • Nationals of EU Member States or Horizon Europe Associated Countries, or
  • Long-term residents of those countries.

Have a look at the Postdoctoral Fellowships page and key resources for an introduction to the scheme’s characteristics and opportunities.

Consult the  MSCA Work Programme and annexes for all details about the call conditions.

Additional Mobility Opportunities

Both European and Global Fellowships may include:

Short-Term Secondments

Researchers may undertake secondments almost anywhere in the world to:

  • Collaborate with external institutions
  • Access specialist facilities
  • Develop new research techniques
  • Strengthen international partnerships

For Global Fellowships, secondments cannot occur during the mandatory return phase.

Non-Academic Placements

To encourage collaboration beyond universities, fellows may receive additional support for placements lasting up to:

  • 6 months

These placements must take place at the end of the fellowship in a non-academic organisation based in:

  • EU Member States
  • Horizon Europe Associated Countries

Examples include:

  • Companies
  • SMEs
  • Government organisations
  • NGOs
  • Innovation centres
  • Research-based industries

Research Areas Supported

One of the strengths of the MSCA programme is that it welcomes proposals from all academic disciplines.

Eligible fields include, but are not limited to:

  • Natural Sciences
  • Engineering
  • Technology
  • Computer Science
  • Artificial Intelligence
  • Medical Sciences
  • Life Sciences
  • Agriculture
  • Environmental Sciences
  • Climate Change
  • Mathematics
  • Economics
  • Education
  • Humanities
  • Arts
  • Social Sciences
  • Law
  • Business
  • Public Policy

Research covered under the Euratom Research and Training Programme is also eligible, subject to additional nationality requirements.

Who Can Apply?

Applicants must submit their proposal jointly with an eligible host organisation.

Host organisations may include:

  • Universities
  • Research institutes
  • Businesses
  • Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs)
  • Public organisations
  • Private organisations
  • Innovation centres
  • Other eligible institutions located in an EU Member State or Horizon Europe Associated Country

Eligibility Requirements

Applicants must satisfy all programme requirements.

1. Hold a PhD

Applicants must:

  • Have been awarded a PhD before the application deadline.

Applicants who have successfully defended their doctoral thesis but have not yet formally received their degree are also eligible.

2. Research Experience

Applicants must have:

  • No more than 8 years of research experience following the award of their PhD.

The following periods do not count toward the eight-year limit:

  • Career breaks
  • Time spent outside research
  • Certain periods spent conducting research outside Europe (for eligible reintegrating researchers)

3. Mobility Rule

Applicants must not have:

  • Lived,
  • Worked,
  • Studied, or
  • Carried out their main activity

in the host country for more than 12 months during the 36 months immediately before the application deadline.

This mobility requirement is one of the programme’s defining eligibility criteria.

4. Resubmission Restriction

Beginning with the 2026 call, applications that scored below 80% during the previous year’s evaluation are subject to resubmission restrictions.

Seal of Excellence

Applicants whose proposals score:

  • 85% or higher

but cannot be funded due to budget limitations will receive a Seal of Excellence.

This prestigious recognition demonstrates that the proposal met high-quality standards and may improve opportunities to obtain funding from other sources.

What Does the Fellowship Funding Cover?

The fellowship provides comprehensive financial support throughout the project.

The beneficiary organisation receives EU funding to employ the researcher for the full fellowship period.

Funding includes:

Researcher Allowances

Researchers receive:

  • Living allowance
  • Mobility allowance

Where applicable, additional support is available through:

  • Family allowance
  • Long-term leave allowance
  • Special needs allowance

Institutional Support

Additional funding is provided for:

  • Research activities
  • Scientific training
  • Networking activities
  • Project management
  • Indirect institutional costs

This ensures researchers have access to the resources needed to successfully complete their projects.

How to Apply

Applications are submitted jointly by:

  • The researcher
  • The host organisation

The proposal is submitted by the host institution through the European Commission’s Funding and Tender Opportunities Portal.

APPLY NOW

Steps to Apply

Applicants should:

  1. Identify a suitable host organisation.
  2. Contact a potential supervisor.
  3. Develop a research proposal jointly.
  4. Confirm eligibility requirements.
  5. Prepare all required documents.
  6. Submit the proposal before the deadline.

Start preparing your application to an open funding call published on the Funding and Tender Opportunities Portal

Finding a Host Organisation

Researchers can identify host institutions through:

  • Existing academic networks
  • Professional contacts
  • Universities
  • Research institutes
  • Euraxess hosting offers
  • Other international research job portals

Many organisations publish expressions of interest seeking researchers for specific research topics.

Check the list of hosting offers (expressions of interest) published on Euraxess

Documents Available for Applicants

The European Commission provides several resources to help applicants prepare competitive proposals.

These include:

  • MSCA Work Programme
  • General Annexes
  • Guide for Applicants
  • Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
  • Application Forms
  • Evaluation Forms
  • Guidelines for calculating the 8-year research experience
  • Self-assessment tool
  • Online application manuals

For more information on the application and evaluation process and contact points for further guidance

  • visit our page on how to apply
  • visit the European Research Executive Agency website

Responsibilities of the Host Organisation

If awarded funding, the host institution will:

  • Recruit the researcher
  • Supervise the fellowship
  • Provide research facilities
  • Deliver training
  • Support networking opportunities
  • Arrange secondments where appropriate
  • Submit the application on behalf of the researcher

Why This Fellowship Stands Out

The MSCA Postdoctoral Fellowships are widely regarded as one of Europe’s most prestigious research funding programmes because they combine:

  • International mobility
  • Career development
  • Scientific excellence
  • Cross-disciplinary collaboration
  • Industry engagement
  • Comprehensive funding
  • High-quality research training
  • Global networking opportunities

Researchers gain access to world-leading institutions while developing skills that significantly enhance future academic and professional careers.

Important Deadline

Application Deadline: 9 September 2026 (17:00 CEST)

Applicants are encouraged to begin preparing well in advance, as developing a strong proposal requires close collaboration with a host organisation and sufficient time to prepare all supporting documents.

Conclusion

The Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions Postdoctoral Fellowships 2026 represent an exceptional opportunity for PhD holders seeking to elevate their research careers through international mobility, interdisciplinary collaboration, and advanced professional development. By supporting researchers across all disciplines and promoting partnerships between academia and industry, the programme helps cultivate the next generation of global research leaders.

Whether you aspire to conduct groundbreaking research in Europe, gain international experience abroad before returning to Europe, or expand your professional network across sectors, the MSCA Postdoctoral Fellowships provide the funding, mentorship, and institutional support needed to achieve those ambitions. Eligible researchers are encouraged to identify a suitable host institution, prepare a competitive proposal, and submit their application before the 9 September 2026 deadline.

Visit the OFY website for more fellowship opportunities.

The post Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions (MSCA) Postdoctoral Fellowships 2026: Fully Funded Research Fellowships in Europe for International PhD Researchers appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Workday Foundation Human Connection Microgrant Program 2026: Apply for Up to $10,000 to Build Stronger, More Connected Communities
item.dc:creator: Dora Kumi
item.description:
In today’s increasingly digital world, people are more connected through technology than ever before. Yet, paradoxically, loneliness and social isolation continue to rise across all age groups. Recognizing this growing global challenge, the Workday Foundation has launched the Human Connection Microgrant Program 2026, an innovative funding opportunity designed to support nonprofit organizations that create meaningful, […]

The post Workday Foundation Human Connection Microgrant Program 2026: Apply for Up to $10,000 to Build Stronger, More Connected Communities appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
In today’s increasingly digital world, people are more connected through technology than ever before. Yet, paradoxically, loneliness and social isolation continue to rise across all age groups. Recognizing this growing global challenge, the Workday Foundation has launched the Human Connection Microgrant Program 2026, an innovative funding opportunity designed to support nonprofit organizations that create meaningful, joyful, and in-person community experiences.

Through grants of up to $10,000 USD, the foundation aims to empower grassroots organizations, charities, and nonprofits to develop projects that bring neighbors together, strengthen relationships, reduce loneliness, and foster long-lasting community connections.

If your organization is passionate about building stronger communities through shared experiences, this grant could provide the financial support needed to turn your ideas into reality.

About the Workday Foundation Human Connection Microgrant Program

The Workday Foundation Human Connection Microgrant Program is a new initiative dedicated to investing in in-real-life (IRL) human connection. Rather than funding traditional service delivery alone, the program supports creative community projects where human connection is intentionally designed into every aspect of the experience. The foundation believes that joyful social interactions create what it calls “joyful feedback loops.” These positive experiences encourage participants to return, invite others, and gradually build stronger, healthier, and more connected neighborhoods.

The program recognizes that genuine relationships cannot always be built online. Instead, they flourish when people gather face-to-face, share experiences, and develop trust over time.

Why the Program Matters

Research continues to show alarming trends in loneliness around the world:

  • One in six adults globally reports experiencing loneliness.
  • One in three adolescents experiences loneliness.
  • Communities are seeing fewer opportunities for spontaneous in-person interactions.
  • Modern lifestyles increasingly reduce opportunities for neighbors to meet naturally.

Several factors have contributed to this trend, including:

  • Remote work
  • Online learning
  • Smartphones
  • Social media
  • Streaming entertainment
  • Delivery services
  • Self-checkout systems
  • Reduced use of public gathering spaces

Although technology has made communication easier, it has also reduced opportunities for meaningful face-to-face relationships.

The Workday Foundation believes communities become healthier when people intentionally create spaces where neighbors can meet, interact, build friendships, and develop trust.

Program Vision

The Human Connection Microgrant Program seeks to fund projects that:

  • Create opportunities for genuine in-person interactions.
  • Build lasting friendships among neighbors.
  • Encourage trust and belonging.
  • Reduce loneliness.
  • Inspire continued participation through enjoyable experiences.
  • Strengthen neighborhoods one connection at a time.

The foundation is particularly interested in projects where social connection is the primary purpose, rather than a secondary outcome.

Types of Projects the Grant Will Support

The Workday Foundation welcomes a wide variety of creative ideas that help people connect.

Examples include:

1. Community Block Parties

Organizations may organize neighborhood block parties that encourage residents to meet, socialize, and celebrate together.

These gatherings should be:

  • Welcoming
  • Inclusive
  • Easy to attend
  • Designed for all ages

2. Community Potlucks and Cultural Celebrations

Projects can include:

  • Shared meals
  • Cultural festivals
  • Seasonal celebrations
  • Story-sharing events
  • Food exchange programs

These activities allow people from different backgrounds to connect through food, traditions, and conversation.

3. Creative Community Projects

The foundation encourages artistic experiences that naturally bring people together, including:

  • Public murals
  • Neighborhood art installations
  • Storytelling walks
  • Music sessions
  • Community choirs
  • Line dancing classes
  • Open creative workshops

4. Games and Recreational Activities

Shared play can become an effective tool for community building.

Examples include:

  • Board game nights
  • Trivia competitions
  • Neighborhood scavenger hunts
  • Community sports
  • Family game festivals

5. Volunteer and Service Projects

Organizations may organize projects where neighbors work together toward a common goal, such as:

  • Community cleanups
  • Park improvement initiatives
  • Neighborhood beautification
  • Local volunteer events

These activities strengthen relationships while improving communities.

6. Intergenerational Programs

The grant encourages activities that connect people of different generations.

Projects may involve:

  • Youth
  • Adults
  • Seniors

Examples include:

  • Story-sharing sessions
  • Community gardening
  • Mentorship activities
  • Shared learning events

7. Outdoor Community Activities

Outdoor recreation provides another opportunity for meaningful interaction.

Eligible ideas include:

  • Hiking groups
  • Camping events
  • Walking clubs
  • Run clubs
  • Outdoor games
  • Nature exploration programs.

What Makes a Strong Project?

Successful proposals should clearly demonstrate how their activities will:

  • Build stronger social relationships.
  • Create welcoming environments.
  • Encourage participation from people outside the organization’s existing network.
  • Foster joy and belonging.
  • Encourage participants to return for future activities.
  • Expand community engagement over time.

The activity itself should revolve around strengthening relationships rather than simply hosting an event.

Who Is Eligible to Apply?

Organizations must meet all of the following requirements:

  • Be a registered nonprofit or charity.
  • Be a 501(c)(3) organization in the United States or a registered charity internationally.
  • Organizations using a fiscal sponsor may apply.
  • Maintain an annual operating budget below USD 500,000.
  • Be eligible to legally receive grant funding.
  • Be in good standing with relevant authorities.

Grassroots organizations and small community organizations are especially encouraged to apply.

Eligible Projects

Projects must:

  • Take place in one of the eligible cities.
  • Focus primarily on building in-person human connection.
  • Be open to the wider community.
  • Encourage participation from neighbors.
  • Be delivered during the grant period.
  • Be either: A single event
    • A series of events
    • An ongoing community program.

Projects That Are Not Eligible

The program will not fund:

  • Virtual-only events
  • Online communities
  • Building construction
  • Facility renovations
  • Capital improvement projects
  • General organizational operating expenses
  • Political campaigns
  • Lobbying activities
  • Religious proselytization
  • Projects focused only on social services without emphasizing human connection
  • Closed clubs or member-only groups
  • For-profit businesses
  • Projects outside the designated cities.

Grant Amount

Successful applicants may receive funding of up to:

Up to USD 10,000

There is no minimum funding request, allowing organizations to seek support according to the scale of their proposed project.

Final award amounts will depend on factors such as:

  • Number of applications received
  • Quality of the proposal
  • Expected community impact
  • Organizational experience
  • Number of people expected to benefit.

Application Requirements

Applicants must complete a brief application including:

Organization Background

Applicants should provide:

  • Mission statement
  • Organizational history
  • Why the organization was established

 

Interested applicants should click HERE to apply

Learn more about the opportunity HERE 

Project Hypothesis

Applicants must explain:

  • How the proposed project will strengthen human connections.
  • Why they believe it will reduce loneliness and build community.

Project Overview

The proposal should include:

  • Description of activities
  • Whether the project is a one-time event or multiple events
  • How new participants will be welcomed
  • Community engagement strategy

Proposed Budget

Organizations should submit a detailed budget outlining:

  • Expected expenses
  • Funding allocation
  • Overall project costs

Measurement and Evaluation Plan

Applicants must explain how they will measure success.

Possible evaluation areas include:

  • Human connection
  • Sense of belonging
  • Reduced loneliness
  • Participant satisfaction
  • Community engagement

Applicants should also submit sample survey questions that will be used to evaluate project impact.

Reporting Requirements

Organizations receiving funding must submit a one-page final report within 60 days after completing their project.

The report should include:

  • Program summary
  • Activities completed
  • Participation numbers
  • Outcomes achieved
  • Photos or videos documenting events

Organizations are also encouraged to use the hashtag #IRLhumanconnection in related social media posts.

Program Timeline

Milestone Date
Applications Open May 28, 2026
Application Deadline July 31, 2026 (11:59 PM Pacific Time)
Application Review August 1 – September 10, 2026
Grant Awards Announced September 21, 2026
Grant Period Begins Upon award notification
Grant Period Ends June 30, 2027
Final Report Due Within 60 days after project completion

Why Organizations Should Apply

This microgrant provides much more than financial support. It offers nonprofit organizations an opportunity to:

  • Address loneliness within their communities.
  • Strengthen neighborhood relationships.
  • Foster inclusion and belonging.
  • Create sustainable community engagement models.
  • Pilot innovative ideas for social connection.
  • Build partnerships across diverse groups.
  • Demonstrate measurable community impact.
  • Contribute to a global movement promoting stronger human relationships.

Whether your organization plans a neighborhood festival, community art project, volunteer initiative, outdoor recreation program, or cultural celebration, the Workday Foundation is looking for ideas that place human connection at the heart of every activity.

Conclusion

The Workday Foundation Human Connection Microgrant Program 2026 represents a significant opportunity for nonprofits and community organizations committed to creating stronger, healthier, and more connected neighborhoods. As loneliness continues to emerge as one of today’s most pressing social challenges, this initiative encourages organizations to design joyful, inclusive, and meaningful experiences that bring people together in real life.

With grants of up to $10,000 USD, the program supports innovative grassroots initiatives that prioritize genuine human interaction over digital engagement. If your organization has a creative idea capable of fostering trust, friendship, and belonging within your community, this funding opportunity could help transform that vision into lasting impact.

For more Global updates on GRANTS click HERE

 

The post Workday Foundation Human Connection Microgrant Program 2026: Apply for Up to $10,000 to Build Stronger, More Connected Communities appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships 2026 in Europe | Fully Funded Research Infrastructure Fellowship
item.dc:creator: Evelyn Essuman
item.description:
Applications are now open for the EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships, an exciting fully funded postdoctoral programme offered by the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL). Designed for outstanding early-career researchers, ARISE2 combines cutting-edge research with professional development, enabling fellows to build successful careers in research infrastructures across Europe. Unlike traditional postdoctoral fellowships, ARISE2 focuses on preparing scientists […]

The post EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships 2026 in Europe | Fully Funded Research Infrastructure Fellowship appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Applications are now open for the EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships, an exciting fully funded postdoctoral programme offered by the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL). Designed for outstanding early-career researchers, ARISE2 combines cutting-edge research with professional development, enabling fellows to build successful careers in research infrastructures across Europe.

Unlike traditional postdoctoral fellowships, ARISE2 focuses on preparing scientists for leadership roles in research infrastructures. Fellows work on innovative technology or method development projects while receiving specialized training in project management, innovation, service delivery, entrepreneurship, and scientific leadership.

If you are passionate about advancing scientific technologies while developing the skills needed to support world-class research facilities, the EMBL ARISE2 Fellowship offers an exceptional career opportunity.

About the EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships

The Career Accelerator for Research Infrastructure Scientists (ARISE2) is the successor to the highly successful ARISE programme at EMBL.

The programme recruits talented postdoctoral researchers to work for three years at one of EMBL’s research sites. During the fellowship, participants undertake technology or method development projects within EMBL research groups and core facilities while participating in a structured professional development programme.

ARISE2 aims to train the next generation of research infrastructure scientists by combining scientific excellence with practical leadership skills, ensuring fellows are well-equipped to contribute to international research infrastructures and scientific innovation.

Fellowship Benefits

Successful fellows will receive:

  • Fully funded three-year postdoctoral appointment
  • Competitive salary and employee benefits
  • Hands-on experience at one of EMBL’s research sites
  • Access to world-class research infrastructure and facilities
  • Professional training in:
    • Leadership
    • Project management
    • Technology development
    • Innovation
    • Entrepreneurship
    • Service delivery
  • International networking opportunities
  • Career development support
  • Collaboration with leading scientists and technology experts across Europe

Research Areas

Fellows will contribute to technology and method development projects in research infrastructures across various life science disciplines.

Projects may involve:

  • Advanced imaging technologies
  • Bioinformatics
  • Structural biology
  • Genomics
  • Molecular biology
  • Data science
  • Artificial intelligence applications in biology
  • Microscopy
  • Proteomics
  • Scientific instrumentation
  • Computational biology

Eligibility Criteria

Applicants should:

  • Hold a PhD (or have completed all requirements before starting the fellowship).
  • Demonstrate strong research achievements.
  • Be interested in technology or method development.
  • Have excellent communication and teamwork skills.
  • Be motivated to pursue careers in research infrastructures.
  • Meet the mobility and eligibility requirements outlined by EMBL.

Researchers from around the world are encouraged to apply, provided they satisfy the programme’s eligibility requirements.

Programme Structure

ARISE2 Fellows are employed for three years at one of EMBL’s research sites.

Each fellow will:

  • Join an EMBL research group or core facility.
  • Conduct a technology or method development project.
  • Receive advanced career development training.
  • Participate in innovation and entrepreneurship activities.
  • Learn project and service management.
  • Develop leadership skills for research infrastructures.

The programme combines scientific research with professional skills that prepare fellows for leadership positions beyond traditional academic careers.

Why Apply?

The EMBL ARISE2 Fellowship offers much more than research funding.

Participants gain:

  • International research experience
  • Practical technology development expertise
  • Leadership training
  • Innovation and entrepreneurship skills
  • Exposure to Europe’s leading life science research infrastructure
  • Long-term career development support

The fellowship is particularly valuable for researchers interested in scientific services, advanced technologies, and research infrastructure management.

How to Apply

Interested applicants should carefully review the programme requirements before preparing their applications.

Applications must be submitted through the official EMBL application system before the published deadline.

Applicants are encouraged to prepare all required documents well in advance and ensure they meet the eligibility criteria before submission.

Official Programme Page:
https://www.embl.org/about/info/arise/

Application Portal:
https://www.embl.org/about/info/arise/apply/

Why Choose EMBL?

EMBL is one of the world’s leading research organizations in molecular biology and life sciences. With multiple research sites across Europe, EMBL provides scientists with access to cutting-edge facilities, interdisciplinary collaboration, and an international research environment.

Through ARISE2, fellows become part of a vibrant scientific community dedicated to advancing research technologies while developing the next generation of research infrastructure leaders.

Conclusion

The EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships provide an outstanding opportunity for postdoctoral researchers seeking to combine scientific excellence with leadership development in research infrastructures. By offering a fully funded position, world-class mentoring, and professional training, the programme prepares fellows for impactful careers at the forefront of scientific innovation.

Researchers interested in technology development, interdisciplinary collaboration, and research infrastructure are encouraged to apply and take advantage of this unique opportunity at EMBL.

Discover more fellowship opportunities like this HERE

The post EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships 2026 in Europe | Fully Funded Research Infrastructure Fellowship appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program 2026: Fully Funded Eight-Week Placement in Perth, Australia
item.dc:creator: Muhammad Chishti
item.description:
Applications Open for the Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program 2026 The Blue Carbon Hub is accepting applications for the 2026 Early Career Ocean Professionals Program, an intensive international placement designed to strengthen the skills, knowledge and professional networks of emerging ocean professionals from Indian Ocean Rim Association Member States. Successful participants will […]

The post Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program 2026: Fully Funded Eight-Week Placement in Perth, Australia appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Applications Open for the Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program 2026

The Blue Carbon Hub is accepting applications for the 2026 Early Career Ocean Professionals Program, an intensive international placement designed to strengthen the skills, knowledge and professional networks of emerging ocean professionals from Indian Ocean Rim Association Member States.

Successful participants will travel to Perth, Western Australia, to undertake a focused project with the Blue Carbon Hub at the Indian Ocean Marine Research Centre. The program will run from 5 October to 27 November 2026, providing approximately eight weeks for participants to develop and complete projects connected to blue carbon science, finance or policy.

The initiative is intended to produce practical knowledge and recommendations that can help Indian Ocean Rim Association Member States protect, restore and sustainably manage blue carbon ecosystems. Participants will be expected to work closely with the Blue Carbon Hub and, where applicable, a nominated project collaborator throughout the placement.

Why Blue Carbon Ecosystems Matter

Blue carbon ecosystems are coastal and marine environments that capture, store and manage significant quantities of carbon. They are closely connected with environmental protection, economic security, climate resilience and the wellbeing of coastal communities.

According to the Blue Carbon Hub’s official call, these ecosystems contribute to:

  • Food security;
  • Sustainable livelihoods;
  • Climate change mitigation;
  • Climate change adaptation;
  • Reduced disaster risk;
  • Coastal resilience;
  • Protection from coastal hazards; and
  • Long-term environmental sustainability.

When blue carbon ecosystems are degraded, they may shift from functioning as carbon sinks to becoming major sources of greenhouse gas emissions. Their deterioration can therefore intensify climate risks while weakening the natural and economic systems upon which coastal communities depend.

Strengthening scientific knowledge, developing effective public policies, creating appropriate governance frameworks and identifying sustainable financial pathways are therefore essential for their protection and restoration. The Early Career Ocean Professionals Program responds to these needs by bringing emerging professionals into a practical environment where they can contribute research, analysis and policy-oriented solutions.

Program Location and Duration

The 2026 program will be hosted by the Blue Carbon Hub at the Indian Ocean Marine Research Centre in Perth, Australia.

Important program dates

  • Application deadline: Midnight on 7 August 2026
  • Notification of application outcome: By 31 August 2026
  • Program starting date: 5 October 2026
  • Program ending date: 27 November 2026
  • Expected commitment: Eight weeks in Australia

The online application form specifically asks applicants whether they can commit to eight weeks in Australia. It provides response options for applicants who can attend the full period, cannot attend or are available for six weeks or less. Applicants should therefore assess their availability carefully before submitting an application.

What Participants Will Do During the Program

Selected early-career professionals will undertake a focused, self-directed project with the Blue Carbon Hub. The project should fall within one of the program’s three main areas:

  1. Blue carbon science
  2. Blue carbon finance
  3. Blue carbon policy

Applicants are encouraged to propose their own innovative project ideas rather than selecting from a predetermined list of assignments. Projects may involve original analysis, synthesis of existing knowledge, preparation of policy recommendations or other practical outputs relevant to blue carbon protection and restoration.

The application form requires applicants to identify whether their proposed project falls under science, finance or policy. Applicants must also provide a project title, a detailed project description and an explanation of how the proposed work will be broadly relevant to IORA Member States.

Types of Projects That May Be Proposed

The program allows a variety of project formats, provided that the proposed activity is relevant to the program’s blue carbon themes and can realistically be completed during the placement.

Projects may include:

  • Original scientific research;
  • Reviews of academic or technical literature;
  • Policy briefs;
  • Finance-related analysis;
  • Governance or institutional studies;
  • Evaluation of blue carbon conservation strategies;
  • Development of practical recommendations;
  • Comparative analysis across IORA Member States;
  • Limited field-based activities;
  • Data analysis;
  • Preparation of regional knowledge products; or
  • Other innovative activities connected with blue carbon science, finance or policy.

The official guidance emphasizes that projects should be focused, achievable and designed to produce meaningful outcomes within the eight-week placement period. Participants are expected to complete their projects while working with the Blue Carbon Hub.

Potential Blue Carbon Project Areas

Although the official call does not prescribe a fixed list of project topics, applicants may consider developing proposals related to areas such as:

  • Conservation and restoration of mangroves;
  • Seagrass ecosystem protection;
  • Coastal wetland management;
  • Blue carbon measurement and accounting;
  • Climate finance for coastal restoration;
  • Community-based conservation models;
  • Governance of blue carbon ecosystems;
  • Coastal resilience and disaster-risk reduction;
  • Sustainable livelihoods connected with coastal resources;
  • Policy frameworks for ecosystem restoration;
  • Regional blue carbon collaboration;
  • Financing mechanisms for conservation projects;
  • Integration of blue carbon into national climate commitments;
  • Evaluation of restoration initiatives; and
  • Institutional capacity for blue carbon policy implementation.
